heading: Gruesse und Anrede: Guten Morgen, Guten Tag
text:
Greetings are essential in German culture. In formal settings, use 'Guten Morgen' (before 11 AM), 'Guten Tag' (midday to evening), 'Guten Abend' (evening), and 'Gute Nacht' (bedtime). Germans place high importance on politeness and proper address. You'll encounter 'du' (informal, for friends/family) and 'Sie' (formal, for strangers/superiors). Notice that 'Sie' is always capitalized, even in the middle of sentences, showing respect through capitalization—a uniquely German feature.

In Bavaria and Austria, 'Gruss Gott' is common instead of 'Guten Tag'. Germans typically shake hands when greeting formally. Even in casual settings, proper greetings set the tone for respectful communication.

glossary.definition: greeting (masculine; plural: die Gruesse)
good morning (using accusative neuter singular 'guten')
form of address; how you address someone
informal 'you' (singular); used with friends, family, children
formal 'you' (singular or plural); always capitalized; used with strangers, elders, superiors
to use 'du' with someone (informal); to address informally
to use 'Sie' with someone (formal); to use the polite form of address

highlight-box: German capitalizes all nouns AND the formal 'Sie', showing respect through grammar.
'du' vs. 'Sie' is crucial: mix them up and you'll seem rude. When in doubt, use 'Sie'.
practice: Practice saying 'Guten Morgen' vs. 'Guten Tag' at different times of day. When would you use 'Sie' vs. 'du'?

heading: Das Genus: der, die, das und Artikel
text:
Every German noun has a grammatical gender: masculine (der), feminine (die), or neuter (das). Unlike English, German articles change based on gender and case. In the nominative case (the subject), we use: 'der Mann' (the man—masculine), 'die Frau' (the woman—feminine), 'das Kind' (the child—neuter). There are no perfect rules for predicting gender, so you must learn each noun with its article. However, some patterns exist: nouns ending in '-er' tend to be masculine (der Lehrer, teacher), '-in' tend to be feminine (die Lehrerin, female teacher), and '-chen'/'-lein' are always neuter (das Maedchen, girl).

Always write nouns with their article and memorize them as a unit. This habit prevents countless errors later when cases change the article form.

glossary.definition: grammatical gender (masculine, feminine, or neuter)
article (definite: the; indefinite: a/an)
the man (masculine noun in nominative case)
the woman (feminine noun in nominative case)
the child (neuter noun in nominative case)
nominative case: the subject of a sentence
the girl (neuter, despite referring to a female)

highlight-box: German has three genders: der (M), die (F), das (N). Learn each noun with its article from day one.
Neuter 'das' often applies to diminutives (-chen, -lein) and some abstract nouns, even if they refer to females.
practice: List 10 common nouns with their correct articles: der, die, or das. Can you spot any patterns?
Why is 'das Maedchen' neuter even though it means 'girl'? (Hint: it's a diminutive of 'die Magd'.)

heading: Nominativ und Akkusativ: Nominative and Accusative Cases
text:
German has four cases, and we'll start with nominative and accusative. The nominative case identifies the subject (who/what does the action): 'Der Mann trinkt Wasser' (The man drinks water—Mann is nominative). The accusative case marks the direct object (who/what receives the action): 'Ich sehe den Mann' (I see the man—Mann is now accusative, so the article changes from 'der' to 'den'). Notice that the article changes based on the case and gender: masculine nouns in accusative take 'den' instead of 'der', but feminine, neuter, and plural articles remain the same. This pattern is one of the most frequent in German, so practice it thoroughly.

Memorize this key distinction: nominative = subject, accusative = direct object. Most verbs, like 'sehen' (to see), 'essen' (to eat), and 'trinken' (to drink), take accusative objects.

glossary.definition: nominative case: identifies the subject of the sentence
accusative case: marks the direct object
subject: the one performing the action
object: the one receiving or affected by the action
accusative article for masculine singular nouns (changes from 'der')
to drink (takes accusative object: Wasser, Milch, Bier)

highlight-box: Nominative = subject (wer/was). Accusative = direct object (wen/was). Only masculine articles change.
Masculine accusative 'den' is the first case ending you'll master. All others stay the same in accusative.
practice: Convert these nominative sentences to accusative: (1) 'Der Lehrer kommt.' → 'Ich sehe...' (2) 'Das Buch ist neu.' → 'Ich lese...'
Why doesn't the feminine or neuter article change in accusative, but masculine does?

heading: Verben im Praesens: Regular Verb Conjugation
text:
German regular verbs (schwache Verben) follow predictable patterns in the present tense. Take the infinitive 'spielen' (to play), remove the '-en', and add endings: ich spiele, du spielst, er/sie/es spielt, wir spielen, ihr spielt, sie/Sie spielen. The verb agrees with the subject in person and number. Notice that the stem 'spiel-' stays the same, and we only change the ending. This becomes automatic with practice. Other common regular verbs include 'machen' (to do/make), 'sagen' (to say), 'fragen' (to ask), 'antworten' (to answer), 'arbeiten' (to work), and 'wohnen' (to live). A few regular verbs need extra care: those ending in '-t' or '-d' (like 'arbeiten': ich arbeite, du arbeitest) insert an 'e' before the consonant for pronunciation.

Regular verbs are predictable, which makes them ideal for building confidence. Master their conjugation pattern, and you'll recognize them in any text.

glossary.definition: infinitive: the base form of a verb (always ends in '-en': spielen, machen)
conjugation: changing the verb based on subject (ich, du, er, etc.)
to play (regular verb; root: spiel-)
to do, to make (regular verb; common, flexible meaning)
stem: the part of the verb that stays the same (spielen → spiel-)
ending: the part that changes with the subject (-e, -st, -t, -en, etc.)

highlight-box: Remove '-en' from the infinitive to find the stem, then add the correct ending for the subject.
For verbs ending in '-d' or '-t', add an extra '-e-' (arbeite, arbeitest) to keep them pronounceable.
practice: Conjugate 'arbeiten' (to work) for all six persons: ich, du, er/sie/es, wir, ihr, sie/Sie.
Why does 'arbeiten' need an extra '-e-' in some forms, but 'spielen' doesn't?

heading: Unregelmaessige Verben: sein und haben
text:
Irregular verbs (starke Verben) don't follow standard patterns and must be memorized. The most important are 'sein' (to be) and 'haben' (to have). 'Sein' is especially irregular: ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie/Sie sind. 'Haben' is more regular but still unique: ich habe, du hast, er/sie/es hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben. Both verbs are essential; 'sein' appears constantly in identification ('Ich bin Anna') and descriptions ('Das Buch ist interessant'), while 'haben' expresses possession ('Ich habe einen Hund') and forms the present perfect tense ('Ich habe gespielt—I have played/I played'). These two verbs appear in nearly every German sentence, so investing time to memorize them now pays enormous dividends.

Practice 'sein' and 'haben' daily until they become automatic. Your fluency depends on them.

glossary.definition: to be (highly irregular: bin, bist, ist, sind, seid, sind)
to have (somewhat irregular: habe, hast, hat, haben, habt, haben)
I am (1st person singular of 'sein')
he is (3rd person singular of 'sein')
I have (1st person singular of 'haben')
present perfect tense; formed with haben/sein + past participle

highlight-box: Sein and haben are so irregular they must be memorized. There are no shortcuts.
'Sein' = identity/state; 'haben' = possession/auxiliary for present perfect. Both appear constantly.
practice: Conjugate both 'sein' and 'haben' for all six persons without looking. Can you do it from memory?
Write 6 sentences: 3 using 'sein' (e.g., 'Ich bin Student') and 3 using 'haben' (e.g., 'Du hast ein Buch').

heading: Mehr unregelmaessige Verben: gehen, sehen, essen
text:
Beyond 'sein' and 'haben', many high-frequency verbs are irregular. 'Gehen' (to go) is crucial: ich gehe, du gehst, er/sie/es geht, wir gehen, ihr geht, sie/Sie gehen. Notice the vowel change from 'e' to 'ie' in the 2nd and 3rd person singular, a pattern called 'stem-vowel change' (Ablaut). 'Sehen' (to see) follows the same pattern: ich sehe, du siehst, er/sie/es sieht. 'Essen' (to eat) also changes its stem vowel: ich esse, du isst, er/sie/es isst. These verbs are high-frequency in everyday conversation—you'll use 'gehen' to describe movement, 'sehen' to talk about observation, and 'essen' for meals. The vowel changes are consistent and logical once you recognize the pattern: e→ie (sehen, gehen), e→i (essen), a→ae (fahren, schlafen). Memorizing these stem-vowel patterns makes learning dozens of other irregular verbs much easier.

Irregular verbs are high-frequency by nature; native speakers use them constantly. Your exposure to them will be enormous, so you'll internalize them naturally with practice.

glossary.definition: to go (stem-vowel change: ich gehe, du gehst, er geht)
to see (stem-vowel change e→ie: ich sehe, du siehst, er sieht)
to eat (stem-vowel change e→i: ich esse, du isst, er isst)
stem-vowel change in irregular verbs (e.g., e→ie, a→ae)
to drive/travel (stem-vowel change a→ae: ich fahre, du faehrst, er faehrt)
to sleep (stem-vowel change a→ae: ich schlafe, du schlaefst, er schlaeft)

highlight-box: Stem-vowel changes (Ablaut) are predictable patterns: e→ie (gehen, sehen), e→i (essen), a→ae (fahren, schlafen).
These high-frequency verbs appear in nearly every conversation. Prioritize memorizing them early.
practice: Conjugate 'gehen', 'sehen', and 'essen' for all six persons. Identify the stem-vowel pattern in each.
Write a short paragraph (4-5 sentences) using 'gehen' (where you go), 'sehen' (what you see), and 'essen' (what you eat).

heading: Dativ: The Indirect Object and Dative Phrases
text:
The dative case (der Dativ) marks the indirect object—the person or thing that receives the benefit or detriment of an action. Compare: 'Ich gebe dem Mann ein Buch' (I give the man—dative—a book). Notice 'dem' replaces 'der' in dative. The dative articles are: der Mann → dem Mann (masculine), die Frau → der Frau (feminine), das Kind → dem Kind (neuter), die Maenner → den Maennern (plural). Certain verbs always require dative objects: 'gefallen' (to please), 'helfen' (to help), 'geben' (to give), 'zeigen' (to show), 'sagen' (to tell). A famous phrase using dative is 'Mir gefaellt das Buch' (The book pleases me). German also uses dative in prepositional phrases: 'mit' (with), 'bei' (at), 'von' (of/by), 'zu' (to), 'in' (in—locative), 'an' (on—locative), 'auf' (on—locative).

The dative case is common in German and appears in many fixed expressions. Learning dative-verb combinations and dative prepositions is essential for fluent conversation.

glossary.definition: dative case: marks the indirect object or object of certain prepositions
dative article for masculine singular nouns (changes from 'der')
dative article for feminine singular nouns (same as nominative)
dative article for neuter singular nouns (changes from 'das')
to please (takes dative: Mir gefaellt...—it pleases me)
with (dative preposition: mit dem Freund—with the friend)

highlight-box: Dative marks the indirect object. Ask 'to whom/for whom' to identify dative in a sentence.
Certain verbs (gefallen, helfen, geben) ALWAYS take dative. Learn them as dative-verb pairs.
practice: Convert these accusative phrases to dative sentences using 'gefallen': (1) 'Ich mag den Hund.' (2) 'Du magst das Buch.' (3) 'Er mag die Pizza.'
List 5 German verbs that require dative objects. For each, write a sample sentence.

heading: Zahlen: Numbers and German Number Customs
text:
German numbers follow clear patterns, but with quirks that English speakers often miss. Units come first, then tens: 21 is 'einundzwanzig' (one-and-twenty), not 'twenty-one'. The '-und' (and) connecting them is mandatory. Numbers 13-19 add '-zehn' to a base: 13 'dreizehn', 14 'vierzehn', 15 'fuenfzehn' (note: drops the final 'f'), 16 'sechzehn' (drops the final 's'), 17 'siebzehn'. Tens are: 20 'zwanzig', 30 'dreissig', 40 'vierzig', 50 'fuenfzig', 60 'sechzig', 70 'siebzig', 80 'achtzig', 90 'neunzig'. Hundreds: 100 'hundert', 200 'zweihundert'. Thousands: 1000 'tausend'. Germans write numbers with periods as thousand separators (1.000) and commas as decimal points (3,14 for pi). In spoken German, use the accusative case with numbers: 'Ich bin einundzwanzig Jahre alt' (I am twenty-one years old).

Mastering German numbers is crucial for daily tasks: telling time, ordering food, discussing prices, and understanding dates.

glossary.definition: number (noun)
twenty-one (literally: one-and-twenty; the '-und' is mandatory)
twenty (first two-digit number)
one hundred
one thousand
age (in the phrase 'Ich bin ... Jahre alt')

highlight-box: German numbers reverse English order: 21 is 'one-and-twenty', not 'twenty-one'. The '-und' is essential.
German uses commas for decimals (3,14) and periods for thousands (1.000)—opposite of English conventions.
practice: Write out these numbers in German words: 13, 25, 47, 68, 99, 156, 234, 2019. Then say them aloud.
Explain why German uses 'eins' (one) as a standalone number but 'ein' in compound numbers like 'einundzwanzig'.

heading: Trennbare Verben: Separable Verbs
text:
German has separable verbs (trennbare Verben)—compounds where the prefix detaches from the stem in conjugated forms. A classic example is 'anfangen' (to begin): ich fange an, du faengst an, er faengt an. Notice 'an-' moves to the end. Other common separable verbs include 'einkaufen' (to shop), 'fernsehen' (to watch TV), 'aufstehen' (to get up), 'abfahren' (to depart). The stress accent falls on the prefix in separable verbs (ANfangen, EINkaufen). In subordinate clauses and infinitive phrases, the prefix rejoins the stem. Example: 'Ich weiss, dass ich morgen frueh aufstehen muss' (I know that I must get up early tomorrow).

Separable verbs are tricky at first, but once you recognize the pattern, they become intuitive.

glossary.definition: separable verb: the prefix detaches in present tense (anfangen → ich fange an)
to begin, to start (prefix: 'an-'; stress: ANfangen)
to shop, to go shopping (prefix: 'ein-'; ich kaufe ein)
to get up, to rise (prefix: 'auf-'; ich stehe auf)
prefix: the part that detaches in separable verbs (an-, ein-, auf-, ab-)
to watch TV (prefix: 'fern-'; ich sehe fern)

highlight-box: In present tense, the prefix moves to the end: 'Ich kaufe Lebensmittel ein' (I shop for groceries).
In subordinate clauses and infinitives, the prefix rejoins the stem: 'Ich muss morgen frueh aufstehen'.
practice: Conjugate 'aufstehen' and 'einkaufen' for all six persons in present tense.
Write two sentences for each of these separable verbs: one in main clause and one in subordinate clause.

heading: Deutsche Esskultur: German Food Culture
text:
German food culture is central to German identity and daily life. The three main meals are 'das Fruehstueck' (breakfast), 'das Mittagessen' (lunch—the largest, warm meal), and 'das Abendessen' (dinner—usually light). Key foods include 'das Brot' (bread), 'die Wurst' (sausage), 'der Kaese' (cheese), and 'das Bier' (beer). The phrase 'Guten Appetit!' (Enjoy your meal!) is common before eating. Regional specialties define German cuisine: Bavarian 'Knoedel' (dumplings), Swabian 'Maultaschen' (German ravioli). Germans take 'Kaffeepause' (coffee break) seriously. Understanding food customs is essential for authentic cultural interaction.

Food vocabulary and mealtime customs are practical necessities for German learners.

glossary.definition: bread (neuter; Germans consume more per capita than almost any nation)
sausage (feminine; hundreds of regional varieties)
cheese (masculine; from many regions)
beer (neuter; deeply tied to German culture)
breakfast (substantial: bread, jam, cheese, cold cuts, coffee)
lunch/midday meal (the largest and warmest meal of the day)
dinner/evening meal (usually light: bread, cheese, cold cuts)

highlight-box: Germans emphasize Brot (bread), Wurst (sausage), and Kaese (cheese). Each region has signature varieties.
'Mittagessen' is the main meal. 'Abendessen' is light. This differs significantly from English-speaking countries.
practice: Describe a typical German breakfast vs. dinner. List 5 foods for each.
Research one German regional specialty. Describe it in German.

heading: Umlaute und Umlautveraenderung: Umlauts and Vowel Shifts
text:
German has three umlaut letters: 'ae' (a-umlaut), 'oe' (o-umlaut), and 'ue' (u-umlaut). These are distinct letters that change pronunciation and meaning. Compare: 'der Mann' (the man) vs. 'die Maenner' (the men). Umlauts appear in plurals, diminutives, and irregular verb conjugations. The umlaut in verb forms follows a pattern: 2nd and 3rd person singular of certain verbs add an umlaut. Examples: fahren (ich fahre, du faehrst, er faehrt), halten (ich halte, du haeltst, er haelt). This pattern is consistent and logical.

Umlauts are fundamental to German and appear constantly.

glossary.definition: umlaut: marks (ae, oe, ue) that change vowel sound and meaning
front unrounded mid vowel
front rounded mid vowel
front rounded high vowel
umlaut vowel shift in irregular verbs and plurals (fahren → faehrst)
little house (diminutive of 'das Haus')

highlight-box: ae, oe, ue are distinct letters. Always write them correctly.
Umlauts appear in verb conjugations, plurals, and diminutives.
practice: Write out these words and explain the difference: (1) der Mann / die Maenner (2) der Fluss / die Fluesse (3) das Haus / die Haeuser
Conjugate fahren, halten, schlafen. Which persons get umlauts?

heading: Plurale und Pluralendungen: German Plural Forms
text:
German plurals use various endings: '-e', '-en', '-er', '-s', and sometimes with umlaut changes. Most feminine nouns add '-en': die Frau → die Frauen. Many masculine nouns add '-e': der Mann → die Maenner (note umlaut). Some neuter and masculine nouns add '-er': das Kind → die Kinder, das Buch → die Buecher. A few modern or foreign nouns add only '-s': das Auto → die Autos. There's no rule to predict which ending each noun takes, so you must learn plurals with each noun from the start. The good news is that all articles in plural are 'die' (regardless of gender).

Practice plurals actively. The variety of plural forms is one of the most challenging aspects of German for learners.

glossary.definition: plural: the form for more than one (all plural articles are 'die')
plural ending: '-e', '-en', '-er', '-s', or no change
the women (plural of 'die Frau'; regular '-en' ending)
the tables (plural of 'der Tisch'; '-e' ending)
the books (plural of 'das Buch'; '-er' ending with umlaut)
the cars (plural of 'das Auto'; '-s' ending)

highlight-box: All plural articles are 'die'. This uniformity simplifies plural recognition.
Learn each noun with its plural from day one: 'der Mann, die Maenner'.
practice: Look up plural forms of these nouns: das Fenster, der Apfel, die Lampe, das Auge, der Anzug. Identify the plural ending.
Write 3 sentences using plural nouns in nominative, then convert to accusative.

heading: Das Perfekt: Present Perfect Tense with haben and sein
text:
The present perfect tense (das Perfekt) describes completed actions in conversational German. It uses a conjugated 'haben' or 'sein' + the past participle. Most verbs use 'haben': 'Ich habe ein Buch gelesen' (I have read a book). The past participle of regular verbs adds 'ge-' prefix and '-t' suffix: spielen → gespielt. Irregular verbs change their stem and add '-en': sehen → gesehen, essen → gegessen, gehen → gegangen. A few verbs use 'sein' instead of 'haben': motion verbs (gehen, fahren) and state-change verbs (aufstehen, einschlafen, werden). The word order in present perfect is: subject + haben/sein + rest of sentence + past participle at the end.

Mastering the present perfect is essential for fluent conversation.

glossary.definition: present perfect tense: haben/sein (conjugated) + past participle
past participle
played (regular past participle of 'spielen': ge-spiel-t)
seen (irregular past participle of 'sehen': ge-seh-en)
gone (irregular past participle of 'gehen': ge-gang-en)
driven/gone (past participle of 'fahren'; uses 'sein')

highlight-box: Regular verbs: ge- + stem + -t (spielen → gespielt). Irregular verbs: ge- + changed stem + -en (sehen → gesehen).
Most verbs use 'haben'; motion/state-change verbs use 'sein' (gehen, fahren, aufstehen, werden).
practice: Form the past participles: lesen, schreiben, sprechen, tanzen, singen, stehen. Identify regular vs. irregular.
Write 6 sentences in present perfect: 3 with haben-verbs and 3 with sein-verbs.

heading: Wortstellung: German Word Order and V2 Rule
text:
German word order is famously complex but logical. The fundamental rule is the V2 principle: the finite verb occupies the second position in a main clause. Examples: 'Ich kaufe Brot ein' (Subject-Verb-Object), 'Morgen fahre ich nach Berlin' (Adverb-Verb-Subject-Object). In subordinate clauses, the word order changes: the verb moves to the end. Example: 'Ich weiss, dass ich morgen nach Berlin fahre' (the verb is at the end). Separable verbs in main clauses have the prefix detached ('Ich kaufe Brot ein'), but in subordinate clauses they rejoin ('dass ich Brot einkaufe'). Negation 'nicht' typically comes after the verb and pronouns. Questions invert the subject and verb: 'Kaufst du Brot ein?'

Recognizing the V2 rule and the main-clause vs. subordinate-clause distinction demystifies German word order.

glossary.definition: word order; German follows the V2 rule in main clauses
finite verb: the conjugated verb in the second position
V2 rule: the finite verb occupies the second position in main clauses
subordinate clause: the finite verb moves to the end
not; negation particle
yes-no question; formed by inverting subject and verb

highlight-box: V2 rule: The conjugated verb is in second position in main clauses. 'Ich kaufe ein Brot.' 'Morgen fahre ich nach Berlin.'
Subordinate clauses are verb-final: 'Ich weiss, dass ich morgen fahre.'
practice: Identify the finite verb position: (1) 'Ich kaufe Brot ein.' (2) 'Morgen fahre ich zur Schule.' (3) 'Weisst du, dass ich morgen fahre?'
Convert these questions to statements: (1) 'Kaufst du Brot ein?' (2) 'Faehrst du morgen nach Berlin?'

heading: Genitiv: The Possessive Case (Introduction)
text:
The genitive case (der Genitiv) expresses possession. In modern German, it's less common in spoken language (speakers often use 'von + dative' instead), but it's essential for written German. The genitive articles are: der Mann → des Mannes (masculine), die Frau → der Frau (feminine), das Kind → des Kindes (neuter), die Maenner → der Maenner (plural). Example: 'Das Buch des Mannes' (The book of the man). Most masculine and neuter singular nouns add a genitive suffix. Certain prepositions require genitive: 'wegen' (because of), 'trotz' (despite), 'waehrend' (during), 'anstatt' (instead of).

The genitive is declining in spoken German, but understanding it is crucial for reading and formal writing.

glossary.definition: genitive case: expresses possession
genitive form of 'der Mann'; adds '-es' or '-s'
genitive form of 'die Frau'; only the article changes
genitive form of 'das Kind'; article 'des' and noun adds '-es' or '-s'
because of (genitive preposition: wegen des Regens—because of the rain)
of + dative (colloquial alternative: das Buch von dem Mann)

highlight-box: Genitive articles: des (M), der (F), des (N), der (plural). Masculine/neuter nouns add '-s' or '-es'.
In spoken German, 'von + dative' often replaces genitive: 'Das Buch von dem Mann'.
practice: Form genitive of these nouns: der Lehrer, die Schule, das Haus, der Student, die Tochter.
Write 4 possession sentences using genitive case.

heading: Adjektive und Adjektivendungen: Adjectives and Agreement
text:
German adjectives must agree with nouns in gender, number, and case. Adjectives can appear before the noun (attributive: 'ein grosses Haus'—a large house) or after a linking verb (predicative: 'Das Haus ist gross'). In predicative position, adjectives take NO ending. In attributive position, they take endings that agree with the noun's gender, number, and case. These depend on whether the adjective follows a definite article, indefinite article, or no article. With definite article: 'der grosse Mann', 'die grosse Frau', 'das grosse Kind'. With indefinite article: 'ein grosser Mann', 'eine grosse Frau', 'ein grosses Kind'. The adjective ending echoes the article in nominative singular.

Adjective endings are a major challenge, but you'll master them through exposure and practice.

glossary.definition: adjective (must agree in gender, number, case when attributive)
attributive adjective: before the noun, takes endings
predicative adjective: after linking verb, no ending
adjective ending: varies by gender, number, case, and article type
larger (comparative form of 'gross')
the largest (superlative form)

highlight-box: Predicative adjectives (after 'ist') take NO ending. Attributive adjectives (before the noun) ALWAYS take endings.
Adjective endings echo the article in nominative singular.
practice: Fill in correct adjective endings: (1) ein klein___ Buch (neuter nominative) (2) die klein___ Buecher (plural nominative) (3) den klein___ Jungen (masculine accusative)
Write 5 sentences with predicative adjectives and 5 with attributive adjectives.

heading: Praeposition: Prepositions and Case Government
text:
Prepositions (die Praepositions) show relationships between nouns. In German, they govern or require specific cases. Accusative prepositions: 'durch' (through), 'fuer' (for), 'gegen' (against), 'ohne' (without), 'um' (around). Example: 'Ich kaufe ein Buch fuer meinen Freund' (I buy a book for my friend). Dative prepositions: 'mit' (with), 'bei' (at), 'von' (of), 'zu' (to), 'nach' (to/after), 'aus' (from), 'seit' (since). Example: 'Ich gehe mit meinem Freund' (I go with my friend). Two-way prepositions take accusative for direction and dative for location: 'in', 'auf', 'an', 'ueber', 'unter', 'neben', 'zwischen', 'vor', 'hinter'. Example: 'Ich gehe in die Schule' (to school—accusative) vs. 'Ich bin in der Schule' (in school—dative).

Mastering preposition case government is one of the most practical investments in German grammar.

glossary.definition: preposition: a word governing a noun/pronoun in a specific case
through (always accusative)
for (always accusative)
with (always dative)
in (accusative for direction, dative for location)
where to? (direction, accusative) / where? (location, dative)

highlight-box: Accusative prepositions: durch, fuer, gegen, ohne, um. Dative prepositions: mit, bei, von, zu, nach, aus, seit, ausser.
Two-way prepositions (in, auf, an, etc.) take accusative for direction and dative for location.
practice: Identify preposition and case: (1) 'Ich gehe mit meinem Freund.' (2) 'Das Buch ist fuer dich.' (3) 'Wir fahren durch die Stadt.'
Fill in correct preposition and case: (1) Ich fahre ___ (for) meinen Freund. (2) Du sitzt ___ (next to) mir.

heading: Fragewoe­rter: German Question Words
text:
Question words (Fragewoe­rter) are essential for conversations. Main question words: 'wer' (who—nominative), 'wen' (whom—accusative), 'wem' (to whom—dative), 'wessen' (whose—genitive), 'was' (what), 'wo' (where—location), 'wohin' (where to—direction), 'woher' (where from—origin), 'wann' (when), 'wie' (how), 'warum' (why), 'welch-' (which), 'wie viel' (how much), 'wie viele' (how many). Examples: 'Wer kommt?' (Who is coming?), 'Wen siehst du?' (Whom do you see?), 'Wem gibst du das Buch?' (To whom do you give the book?). 'Wer/wen/wem/wessen' changes with case. 'Was' doesn't change but serves both nominative and accusative. 'Welch-' is an agreeing adjective taking regular adjective endings. Questions with question words place the question word first, then invert subject and verb: 'Wer kommt morgen?' 'Wann fahrt ihr nach Berlin?'

Mastering question words transforms you into an active conversationalist.

glossary.definition: question word (wer, was, wo, wie, wann, warum, etc.)
who (nominative; changes: wen—acc., wem—dat., wessen—gen.)
what (nominative and accusative; doesn't change)
where (location)
when (time)
why (reason)

highlight-box: 'Wer/wen/wem/wessen' changes with case. 'Was' does not.
'Wo' (location) differs from 'wohin' (direction) and 'woher' (origin).
practice: Create questions: (1) 'Mein Freund kommt morgen.' (Wer...?) (2) 'Ich sehe einen Mann.' (Wen...?) (3) 'Das ist ein Buch.' (Was...?)
Explain the difference between 'wo', 'wohin', and 'woher'. Give examples.

heading: Konjunktionen: Coordinating and Subordinating Conjunctions
text:
Conjunctions (Konjunktionen) connect clauses. Coordinating conjunctions join equal clauses without changing word order: 'und' (and), 'oder' (or), 'aber' (but), 'denn' (for/because), 'sondern' (but rather). Example: 'Ich kaufe Brot und Kaese' (I buy bread and cheese). Subordinating conjunctions introduce dependent clauses with verb-final word order: 'weil' (because), 'dass' (that), 'wenn' (if/when), 'obwohl' (although), 'bevor' (before), 'nachdem' (after), 'waehrend' (while), 'bis' (until). Example: 'Ich weiss, dass du morgen kommst' (the verb is at the end). The word order change is critical: main clauses follow V2; subordinate clauses are verb-final. A subtle distinction: 'denn' (coordinating) doesn't change word order, but 'weil' (subordinating) does. Example: 'Ich gehe nach Hause, denn ich bin muede' vs. 'Ich gehe nach Hause, weil ich muede bin'.

Mastering conjunctions improves your sentence-combining ability and comprehension dramatically.

glossary.definition: conjunction: a word connecting clauses or words
coordinating conjunction (und, oder, aber, denn, sondern)
subordinating conjunction (weil, dass, wenn, obwohl, etc.)
and (coordinating; no word-order change)
because (subordinating; triggers verb-final word order)
for/because (coordinating conjunction; doesn't change word order)

highlight-box: Coordinating conjunctions (und, oder, aber, denn, sondern) maintain V2. Subordinating conjunctions (weil, dass, wenn, obwohl) trigger verb-final.
'Denn' vs. 'weil': both mean 'because', but 'denn' keeps verb in position 2, 'weil' moves it to the end.
practice: Identify coordinating vs. subordinating: (1) 'Ich bleibe zu Hause, denn es regnet.' (2) 'Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil es regnet.'
Combine sentences using coordinating and subordinating conjunctions.

heading: Zusammenfassung: German A1-A2 Essentials
text:
German A1-A2 has equipped you with the foundation for daily communication. You've mastered: the three genders with articles, nominative and accusative cases (plus dative and genitive), regular and irregular verb conjugations in present tense, present perfect for the past, German word order with V2 rule and verb-final subordinate clauses, separable verbs, adjective and preposition agreement, and essential vocabulary covering greetings, numbers, food culture, and question formation. You've internalized umlauts, plural formations, and the nuances of 'du' vs. 'Sie'. You've begun to think in German structures: nominative subjects, accusative objects, dative beneficiaries, verb-final clauses. The next steps are intermediate grammar (modal verbs, conditional tense, passive voice) and thematic vocabulary expansion (work, family, travel, health). Consistent exposure through reading, listening, speaking, and writing will cement these foundations and build fluency. Continue practicing daily, engage with native speakers, consume German media, and celebrate milestones. A1-A2 represents roughly 500-1500 hours of study; B1-B2 (intermediate) requires another 1500+ hours. Viel Erfolg! (Much success!)

Language learning is a marathon. These 20 lessons provide the critical 80% foundation. The remaining 20% comes from engagement with authentic German.

glossary.definition: summary; recap of key concepts
foundation; the base of skills built in A1-A2
fluency; ability to speak/understand naturally
exposure; consistent engagement through reading, listening, speaking
Much success! (common well-wishing phrase)
milestone; significant achievement or marker

highlight-box: A1-A2 provides essential foundation: three genders, cases, verb conjugation, V2 word order, and everyday vocabulary.
Fluency comes from consistent exposure. Celebrate your progress and continue daily practice.
practice: Reflect on your A1-A2 journey: Which topics were easiest? Most challenging? How will you build on this foundation?
Set a B1 goal. What vocabulary and grammar structures will you prioritize? How will you maintain daily German engagement?
